It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 40 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the purple bar didn't change anything (the board looks the same). Let me check if the board actually changed.
I need to fundamentally rethink this puzzle. I've used 40 actions and haven't made any progress toward completing the level. Let me go back to basics and carefully analyze what I know.
Key observations:
1. LEFT/RIGHT moves green squares horizontally (3 cells per action)
2. UP/DOWN moves green squares vertically (but only the left one seems to move)
3. Clicking on a green square changes its dot color (cycle: W → B → c → W)
4. The purple bar blocks horizontal movement
5. The left frame can contain the green square or a white square
6. The right frame contains a white square
Wait, I just realized something important. When I click on the right green square, nothing happens (board_changed: false). But when I click on the left green square, the dot color changes. This suggests the right green square is "locked" or "inactive".
But earlier, when I first started, I was able to click on both green squares to change their dot colors. Let me check if the right green square's dot color can still be changed.
Actually, looking back at my earlier actions, I clicked on the right green square at (31, 31) and the dot color changed from B to W. But now, when I click on the right green square at (31, 22), nothing happens.
The difference might be that the right green square was at (31, 31) when I clicked it, but now it's at (31, 22). Maybe the right green square can only have its dot color changed when it's at a specific position?
Or maybe the issue is that I'm clicking on the wrong cell. Let me check the exact position of the right green square's dot.
Actually, I already checked this earlier. The right green square is at (31, 22) with a c dot, and the dot is at (31, 22). So clicking at (31, 22) should click on the dot.
Wait, but the result said `board_changed: false` and `total_cells_changed: 0`. This means the click didn't register at all, not that the dot color didn't change. Maybe the right green square is not clickable when it's at (31, 22)?
Let me try moving the right green square to a different position and then clicking on it. But I can't move the right green square with LEFT/RIGHT because those actions move the left green square.
Hmm, this is a problem. How do I move the right green square?
Actually, looking back at my earlier actions, I was able to move the right green square with LEFT/RIGHT when the left green square was at its initial position (31, 19). But now, when the left green square is at (34, 19), LEFT/RIGHT moves the left green square.
So the rule might be: LEFT/RIGHT moves the green square that's on row 31 (the middle row). When both green squares are on row 31, it moves the right one. When only one is on row 31, it moves that one. When neither is on row 31, it moves... something else?
Let me test this hypothesis. Currently, the left green square is at (34, 19) (row 34) and the right green square is at (31, 22) (row 31). If I press LEFT/RIGHT, it should move the right green square (since it's on row 31).
Wait, but earlier when the left green square was at (34, 13) (in the frame) and I pressed RIGHT, the left green square moved. So the rule is not simply based on the row.
Actually, I think the rule is: LEFT/RIGHT moves the green square that's not in a frame. When the left green square is in the frame, LEFT/RIGHT moves the right green square. When the left green square is not in the frame, LEFT/RIGHT moves the left green square.
Let me test this. Currently, the left green square is at (34, 19), which is not in the frame (the frame is at (34, 13)). So LEFT/RIGHT should move the left green square. And indeed, when I pressed RIGHT earlier, the left green square moved from (34, 13) to (34, 19).
So to move the right green square, I need to put the left green square in the frame. Let me do that.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check if board changed after clicking purple bar
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed', False))

# Current state: left green at (34, 19), right green at (31, 22)
# To move right green square, I need left green square in the frame
# Move left green square LEFT 2 steps to (34, 13)
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
